---
title: "Chapter 4: Customer and Feeder Clustering"
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

Chapter 2 plotted a handful of AusNet's 342 real households side by side
and made one plain observation: no two profiles agree. Some households
barely draw half a kilowatt all day. Others spike past five kilowatts
every evening. That chart was true, and also incomplete. A {{< acr DSO >}}
cannot plan a feeder, price a tariff, or run a demand-response program
around 342 individual shapes. It needs a smaller number of real,
recurring patterns underneath that mess, not an average that describes no
one.

This chapter builds those patterns from AusNet's own year of real
smart-meter data. It checks whether a customer's pattern is a stable
trait or a one-day snapshot, a question none of this chapter's own
reference papers ask. And it tests whether knowing a customer's pattern
actually predicts anything a {{< acr DSO >}} would care about: which real
{{< acr DER >}} constraint, voltage or thermal, that customer's own
future {{< acr PV >}} or {{< acr EV >}} adoption is likely to trigger.

## Why cluster, not classify

There is no ground-truth "true customer type" label for real households
at this scale. No utility keeps one. That makes this a clustering
problem, an unsupervised search for structure, not a classification
problem with a known answer to check against. Chapter 3's phase-
identification work had a real answer to check its own clustering
against. This chapter does not, and leans on validity indices and honest
sensitivity checks instead.

The second choice that matters is what "similar" means for a load shape.
A small apartment and a large house can have very different absolute
demand and still share the same underlying rhythm, both peaking in the
evening, both quiet at 3 a.m. Clustering on raw magnitude would separate
them by size instead of by behavior. Every profile in this chapter is
normalized by its own peak before clustering, the same "shape, not
magnitude" principle Chapter 3 used for a different reason, voltage
correlation rather than raw voltage tracked phase membership there. Here
it is what lets a small household and a large one land in the same
archetype when their rhythm actually matches.

This is not new ground. McLoughlin, Duffy, and Conlon compared k-means,
k-medoids, and self-organizing maps for exactly this problem, domestic
load-shape characterization, and settled the same habit this chapter
follows below: check more than one validity index, since no single one
reliably picks the right number of clusters alone
[@mcloughlin2015clustering]. Kwac, Flora, and Rajagopal built an encoded
load-shape dictionary from real California smart-meter data to drive five
program-relevant lifestyle segments, an early demonstration that shape-
based clusters map onto real customer behavior, not just statistical
artifacts [@kwac2014segmentation]. Haben, Singleton, and Grindrod
clustered real UK residential demand directly on behavioral shape, the
same instinct this chapter applies to AusNet's own data
[@haben2016clustering].

More recently, Rajabi et al. benchmarked clustering techniques for load-
pattern segmentation directly, the role this chapter's own validity-index
comparison plays for AusNet specifically [@rajabi2020comparative].
Michalakopoulos et al. ran nearly 5,000 real London households through
k-means, k-medoids, hierarchical, and DBSCAN clustering, adding
explainable AI so the resulting archetypes are interpretable,
not just numerically distinct [@michalakopoulos2024clustering]. Yerbury
et al. pushed further still with CROCS, a two-stage framework that first
summarizes each real consumer's own representative daily behaviors, then
clusters consumers by how similar those behavior sets are, tested on real
Australian smart-meter data, the most current entry in this lineage
[@yerbury2026crocs]. None of the five, though, ask whether a customer's
archetype holds up across a full year rather than the one window each one
clusters on, the gap this chapter's own stability check fills.

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

**Shape, not magnitude, is the clustering feature.** Two households can
draw very different absolute power and still share the same underlying
rhythm. A method that clusters on raw magnitude answers "which customers
use a similar amount of power," a question dominated by household size.
A method that clusters on peak-normalized shape answers "which
customers' demand rises and falls together," the question that actually
separates behavioral archetypes. Every result in this chapter clusters
on shape.

:::

![A small apartment (peak 1.0 kW) and a large house (peak 4.5 kW) share the same evening rhythm. Clustering on raw magnitude (left) separates them by size; clustering on peak-normalized shape (right), the choice this chapter makes, groups them together because their rhythm matches.](../../assets/customer-shape-vs-magnitude.svg){#fig-shape-vs-magnitude}

## Getting the data

This chapter reuses the same real AusNet smart-meter pool Chapters 1
through 3 already vendored, no new fetch step needed:

```bash
uv run python scripts/fetch_part4_ausnet_data.py
```

## A real season, not one day

A single day is a noisy read on anyone's routine: a sick day, a weekend,
a one-off late night. Averaging a real customer's own readings across a
season smooths that out while staying specific to that customer, not a
population average.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#5e9c50df >}}

## A baseline worth checking, not assuming

The first step is k-means. Rather than assuming the number of clusters,
this chapter checks it with three validity indices together: the elbow
method on inertia, the silhouette coefficient, and the Davies-Bouldin
index, the same combination McLoughlin et al. and Rajabi et al. recommend
checking together, since no single index reliably picks the right k alone
[@mcloughlin2015clustering; @rajabi2020comparative].

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#c55f2ee6 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#c316f933 >}}

The small-multiples view above puts each index on its own scale. Normalizing
all three to a common 0-1 range and overlaying them makes the same trends
easier to compare directly: does the elbow in inertia line up with the
silhouette peak, or point somewhere else entirely.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#bcee7ce9 >}}

The data show no clear elbow, and the silhouette coefficient peaks at
k=2 (0.227) then decays smoothly. Real residential behavior does not
split into a small number of cleanly separated groups, a genuine finding
about this data, not a modeling failure. k=5 is used from here on, past
the strict silhouette optimum (0.163 at k=5), trading a small amount of
statistical cleanliness for archetypes specific enough to tell a real
story with, a defensible, common choice in applied clustering work, made
transparently rather than silently.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#6405cdb4 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#9459cd85 >}}

Five visually distinct rhythms: a sharp early-evening peaker, a gradual
afternoon-into-evening riser, a late-evening peaker with a small morning
bump, and two broadly similar late-night risers, exactly the pair the
modest silhouette score was warning about. The chart makes concrete what
the number only hinted at: some of these archetypes genuinely overlap.

## How much does that overlap actually matter?

"Some overlap" is a qualitative read on a chart. Turning it into a number
a {{< acr DSO >}} could actually use borrows a tool Chapter 3 already
built: split conformal prediction. There, it checked phase assignments
against a known answer, flagging which customers' labels deserved a
second look. This chapter has no known answer to check against, but the
same tool still works, aimed at a different question: not "is this label
correct," but "how many archetypes could this customer plausibly belong
to."

The method fits k-means on part of the customers and holds the rest back.
For each held-back customer, it measures the distance from that
customer's profile to its own nearest cluster centroid, a real, checkable
number for every customer, not just the ones near a boundary. The held-
back customers' distances pick a threshold, the value 90% of them fall
under. Any customer, old or new, then gets a prediction *set*, not a
single label: every archetype whose centroid sits within that same
threshold.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#69204201 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#ff2c58e3 >}}

Calibrated on 103 held-back customers, the threshold sits at 1.158. Only
90 of 342 customers (26%) get an unambiguous, single-archetype set. Most
get two, three, or even four plausible archetypes. Twenty customers (6%)
get an empty set: their own best-matching archetype still sits farther
from its centroid than 90% of the held-back customers ever did, real
households whose behavior does not confidently match any of the five
archetypes.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#01fefd41 >}}

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

This is the same split conformal machinery Chapter 3 used, aimed at a
different job. There, it flagged phase assignments worth a second look
against a known answer. Here, with no known answer to check against, it
still earns its keep: it turns "some of these archetypes overlap," a
read on a chart, into a checkable, per-customer confidence set. A
{{< acr DSO >}} deciding how much to trust an archetype-based connection
or tariff decision now has a real number to weigh that decision against.

:::

## Does a more advanced method earn its complexity?

IDEC, Improved Deep Embedded Clustering, pretrains an autoencoder, then
jointly optimizes a reconstruction loss and a clustering loss, in
principle learning a representation shaped for clustering rather than
clustering whatever k-means is handed [@guo2017idec]. Kumar and
Mallipeddi apply the same idea to load-pattern segmentation directly,
confirming that lineage is still active [@kumar2024deepclustering].
Worth testing on this exact data before assuming it helps.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#f836f90b >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#78a91d7d >}}

::: {.ark-mistake}
<span class="ark-mistake-title"><i class="bi bi-bug-fill"></i> Common Mistake</span>

**IDEC underperforms plain k-means at every k tested, and the gap widens
as k grows.** This is not a bug. It is what happens when a deep method
meets data too small and low-dimensional to need it: 342 customers, 48
features, is not enough for an autoencoder's extra representational
flexibility to earn its own complexity over a much simpler method already
finding the same structure. The lesson is not "IDEC is bad." It is that a
more advanced method has to prove itself on the data actually in hand,
not get credited for sophistication it does not need here.

:::

## Are these archetypes stable, or a snapshot artifact?

None of this chapter's ten reference papers test this directly. Every one
clusters on a single representative period and treats the result as
fixed. AusNet's own real data is a full year, 365 days per customer, and
most of it goes unused once one season is picked. Re-clustering on
different quarters and comparing the result answers a question none of
the ten papers do: is a customer's archetype a stable trait, or does it
drift?

Checking stability needs a metric first. The Adjusted Rand Index
({{< acr ARI >}}) compares two sets of cluster labels for the same
customers. For every pair of customers, it asks whether both label sets
agree on putting them together or keeping them apart. A score of 1.0
means the two labelings agree on every pair. A score of 0.0 means the
agreement is no better than what random labels would give by chance,
since {{< acr ARI >}} corrects for that chance level. There is no ground
truth to compare against here, so the two labelings being compared are
two independent, real clusterings of the same 342 customers, one built
from each quarter's own data. If a customer's archetype is a stable
trait, the quarter-to-quarter clusterings should agree closely. If
archetypes actually drift over the year, they should not.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#458153e0 >}}

Every quarter clusters about as cleanly as the season used above, so
whatever comes next is not an artifact of picking an unusually messy
period. The real test is whether the same customer lands in a matching
archetype across quarters, not just whether each quarter clusters
cleanly on its own.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#71cb776b >}}

The six pairwise {{< acr ARI >}} scores are a summary. The transition
matrix below shows exactly where the disagreement comes from: which
archetype each customer moves to between the first and the last quarter
of the year.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#7eef3a8e >}}

The {{< acr ARI >}} scores are low, between 0.06 and 0.34. The heatmap
shows why: there is no strong diagonal. If archetypes were a fixed trait,
most customers would keep the same label from Q1 to Q4, filling the
diagonal cells. Instead the counts spread across the grid.

Before treating this as real drift, one thing is worth checking first:
how much do the labels move around on their own, just from re-running
k-means on the *same* quarter with a different random seed?

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#e222a411 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#f230cc27 >}}

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Same-quarter, different-seed agreement sits at 0.96-0.99. K-means is
highly deterministic on this data, ruling out algorithmic noise as the
explanation. Cross-quarter agreement is 0.06-0.34, an order of magnitude
lower, showing two clearly separated clusters of points in the chart
above. The gap between these numbers is the actual finding: this is not
the clustering method being unreliable. Real customers genuinely change
which archetype they belong to as the year moves on. This is not a
stable trait a utility can safely assume holds from one assessment to
the next.

:::

## Can archetype really predict DER risk?

In Chapter 1, three ways {{< acr DER >}} strains a feeder were
identified: fluctuation, load growth, and constraint violations. Chapter
2 showed that {{< acr PV >}} and {{< acr EV >}} adoption bind on
different constraints on this same feeder, voltage first for
{{< acr PV >}}, thermal first for {{< acr EV >}}. If archetype membership
predicts which constraint a customer is likely to trigger, network
planning can shift from reactive, a hosting-capacity study run after a
connection request arrives, to proactive, knowing the risk before it
does.

This is checkable directly. Chapter 2's own `run_penetration()` sampled
its 31-customer subset from this exact 342-customer pool with
`np.random.default_rng(42)`. The same seed reproduces exactly which real
customers that study used, so their archetype labels, from the
342-customer clustering above, and their real simulated risk can be
compared directly, not approximated.

There is a solid physical reason to expect this connection, not just
wishful thinking. An archetype describes a customer's own demand shape.
Voltage rise from {{< acr PV >}} depends on how much power flows backward
through the line at the moment {{< acr PV >}} output is highest, usually
midday. A customer who already draws very little at midday leaves more
room for that export before their own local voltage rises too far.
Thermal loading from {{< acr EV >}} charging depends on how much extra
power lands on the transformer at the moment it is already busiest,
usually the evening peak. A customer whose archetype already peaks hard
in the evening adds an {{< acr EV >}}'s charging load right on top of
their own peak, not into a quiet period. If archetype carried no real
information, none of this would show up in the simulation below. If it
does, archetype becomes a genuine planning signal, not just a label.

Two functions do the work. `build_ausnet_network()` rebuilds the same
feeder Chapters 1 and 2 already solved. `per_customer_pv_voltage_risk()`
sweeps a {{< acr PV >}} scenario across it and returns each customer's
own worst-case bus voltage, not only the feeder-wide maximum Chapter 2
reported. A matching `per_customer_ev_thermal_contribution()` sweeps an
{{< acr EV >}} scenario and returns each customer's own peak kW draw at
the feeder's own peak-loading instant.

Both scenarios use exactly the same seed and the same sampling call as
Chapter 2's own study, so they draw the same 31 real customers.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#8f5ed348 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#ff56b6fd >}}

Archetype 3 carries the highest mean voltage risk (1.1019 per-unit) but
ranks only fourth of five on mean thermal contribution (8.38 kW).
Archetype 2 carries the highest mean thermal contribution (10.61 kW) but
ranks only second on voltage risk (1.0994 per-unit). Different customer
groups lead different risks on the same feeder, mirroring Chapter 2's own
{{< acr PV >}}-versus-{{< acr EV >}} finding, now visible at the
archetype level.

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Archetype membership carries a real signal, and it does not point the
same way for both {{< acr DER >}} types. A {{< acr DSO >}} that only
checks "which archetype is risky" without asking "risky for which
constraint" would still miss half the picture. The sample is small, 31
customers across five archetypes, so this is a real, checkable
association worth pairing with more data before treating it as settled,
not a large-scale statistical claim.

:::

## A controlled test: can clustering discover a new behavior?

Every check so far describes behavior already sitting in the data. A
{{< acr DSO >}}'s real question is forward-looking: if a specific group
of customers started charging an {{< acr EV >}} tomorrow, would re-
running this same clustering actually notice? That is a testable claim,
not a hypothetical one. Take a real {{< acr EV >}} charging profile,
inject it onto a known set of real households, and check whether
unsupervised clustering rediscovers that group on its own, with no label
telling it where to look.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#9182a404 >}}

Add the real {{< acr EV >}} charging shape directly onto each adopter's
own real kW draw, before normalizing, then re-cluster everyone. Two
versions: k=5, the same number of archetypes used throughout this
chapter, and k=6, one extra slot in case the adopters form a genuinely
new shape rather than just relabeling an existing one.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#8a4d3a19 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#adcbd769 >}}

The bar chart shows *where* the adopters landed. It does not show *what*
that cluster's shape actually looks like, or whether it resembles a real
{{< acr EV >}} charging bump rather than an existing archetype's own
evening peak.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#636de7d1 >}}

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Recall is strong: 90% of the injected households land in the same single
cluster at k=5, and 97.5% at k=6. Purity is honest, not perfect: that
cluster is only 38-51% actual adopters, the rest are real households
whose own baseline behavior already looks similar to an evening-charging
{{< acr EV >}} shape. Re-clustering does not perfectly separate every new
adopter from a household that already resembled one, but it reliably
flags where the new behavior concentrates. A {{< acr DSO >}} re-running
this analysis periodically would see this cluster grow and know
something real changed there, even without perfectly identifying every
individual adopter.

:::

## Sensitivity: how much history does one clustering run need?

The stability check above looks at whether an archetype remains
consistent across different situations. But there is another, more
practical question to consider. Within a single season, how much data
does one clustering run actually need to be reliable?

This is important for a {{< acr DSO >}} deciding whether to run this
analysis on just the first week of readings from a new meter instead of
waiting for a full quarter.

They need to know this before considering the stability question. Chapter
3 looked at a similar question about how much data and how many meters
are needed for phase identification. This applies the same approach
here.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#f2c0a5d3 >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#b7dbf2ef >}}

A single day of data hardly matches the season-long baseline. The
{{< acr ARI >}} for one day is 0.02, which is almost random. Even after a
week, the score is still low at 0.11. After a month, the score improves
to 0.35, better, but still not stable. Choosing a season-long window was
not a random decision at the start of this analysis. It marks the point
where the archetype labels stop changing on their own, before the
cross-quarter stability question above even gets asked. A {{< acr DSO >}}
trying to shortcut this analysis with a single day or week of new meter
data would be clustering noise, not behavior.

## Feeder-level clustering

Everything above groups customers. Ledva and Mathieu and Li et al. ask
the same question one level up: do whole feeders fall into a handful of
aggregate archetypes too, residential-heavy, commercial-heavy, mixed,
useful for a {{< acr DSO >}} planning investment across many feeders at
once, not just one at a time [@ledva2020feederdemand; @li2015substation].
AusNet's own single 31-customer feeder cannot answer this, there is only
one of it. This section reuses SMART-DS, {{< acr NREL >}}'s real,
validated synthetic distribution testbed for the Austin, TX region, 33
real {{< acr LV >}} feeders across 8 substations, each with its own real
per-building demand profiles.

Each feeder's network model uses 200 to 350 real customer profiles from a
shared, region-wide pool. Fetching every profile for all 33 feeders would
mean thousands of small downloads, even though this section only needs
each feeder's overall load shape, not every individual customer. Instead,
the fetch script samples a real, kW-stratified subset per feeder from the
same shared pool:

```bash
uv run python scripts/fetch_part4_smartds_data.py
```

Two small helpers do the work: `feeder_load_profiles()` parses a feeder's
own `Loads.dss` into `(customer name, kW, profile id)` triples, and
`feeder_aggregate_shape()` sums whichever sampled profile CSVs actually
landed on disk into one real, kW-weighted aggregate demand curve per
feeder.

Since these are common, real patterns found throughout the region, and
not unique to each customer, even a small sample from each feeder
represents a much larger part of that feeder's customer base than the
sample size alone suggests: samples drawn for other feeders often include
patterns this feeder's own customers also happen to use.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#4f34063a >}}

Coverage varies. Some feeders have thin coverage, only a handful of real
customers whose sampled shape happened to match, worth keeping in mind:
their own cluster membership rests on less real evidence than a feeder
with hundreds of matched customers (median 767, ranging 1 to 2,580), the
same "how many meters do you need" caution Chapter 3's own sensitivity
section raised for customer-level phase identification.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#68718f7c >}}

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#7def7104 >}}

Clustering at the feeder level gives much clearer separation than at the
customer level: a silhouette score of 0.67 at k=2, against 0.23 for
individual customers. This is expected, not a contradiction. Averaging at
the feeder level smooths out the individual noise that caused overlap at
the customer level in the first place. Unlike the customer-level case,
k=2 actually is the validity-index optimum here, not a compromise past
it.

{{< embed 04-customer-feeder-clustering-code.ipynb#895b3642 >}}

Two distinct feeder archetypes emerge. The first shows a sharp evening
peak and much lower daytime demand, consistent with a residential-heavy
mix. The second holds a sustained plateau through the middle of the day
before a noisier, more irregular evening, consistent with a commercial-
heavy mix whose demand does not track the residential dinner-and-bedtime
rhythm the rest of this chapter has been clustering on. Exactly the
"different customer mix" a {{< acr DSO >}} would want to plan for
differently, now visible at the feeder level, not just guessed at.

## Why bother

Chapter 1 named three ways {{< acr DER >}} strains a feeder: fluctuation,
load growth, and constraint violations. Every study since Chapter 2 has
answered "how much strain, and where" one feeder at a time, after the
fact. This chapter's contribution is different: knowing which customers
are likely to cause which kind of strain, before they ever apply to
connect solar or an {{< acr EV >}} charger.

**On the utility side**, the archetype-versus-risk cross-tabulation above
is a leading indicator, not a retrospective one. Archetype 3 flags
voltage risk; archetype 2 flags thermal risk; neither flag would exist
without clustering customers by behavior first. A {{< acr DSO >}}
triaging a connection queue can prioritize monitoring and reinforcement
spend on the archetypes this chapter actually flags as high-risk, and
pair the right lever from Chapter 2, Volt-Watt, Volt-VAr, or storage,
with the right archetype at connection time, instead of a one-size-fits-
all rule applied after a hosting-capacity study finds a problem. The
controlled {{< acr EV >}} injection test adds a second, forward-looking
piece: re-clustering periodically would catch a new adopter cluster
forming with 90-97.5% recall, a real early-warning signal for where
{{< acr DER >}} adoption is concentrating, not just where it already has.

**On the customer side**, the same cross-tabulation cuts the other way. A
customer whose archetype this chapter shows as low-risk can get a faster,
cheaper connection approval. One whose archetype shows real risk can be
paired with the right mitigation up front, storage bundled with the
connection, say, instead of a surprise curtailment discovered months
later. A tariff targeted by real behavioral archetype, not a blanket
residential rate, is a fairer cost-to-serve match for both sides of the
meter.

**The stability finding closes the loop.** This two-sided value only
holds if an archetype-based decision stays valid after it is made. The
conformal confidence sets above already flag which customers' archetype
labels are solid and which are ambiguous at the moment of decision. The
cross-quarter check goes further: it shows the label itself can drift
within a year, 0.06-0.34 agreement against 0.96-0.99 for pure algorithmic
noise. A stable archetype is a trustworthy basis for a connection-
approval or tariff decision that persists. A churning one means last
year's archetype is targeting the wrong households today, a real, costed
risk, repeated re-clustering, or decisions quietly going stale, a
{{< acr DSO >}} would actually have to weigh, not a hypothetical one.

## Where this leaves Part 4

Five real customer archetypes, tested against three separate validity
indices rather than assumed. A deep-clustering contender that honestly
loses to a much simpler method on this data. A conformal confidence set
that turns "some archetypes overlap" into a checkable, per-customer
number. A stability check none of this chapter's ten reference papers
run, showing those archetypes are a real but genuinely time-varying
trait, not a fixed label. A sensitivity check showing why a full season,
not a day or a week, earns that trust. A controlled test showing
clustering can rediscover a real behavior change with 90-97.5% recall.
And a direct, checkable link from archetype to which real {{< acr DER >}}
constraint, voltage or thermal, a customer's own future {{< acr PV >}} or
{{< acr EV >}} adoption is likely to trigger. Two real feeder archetypes
on top of that, the same method one level up.

These archetypes are not the end of the story. They are a grouping key
the rest of Part 4 reuses. Thread 3 builds anomaly-detection baselines
per archetype rather than one baseline for all 342 customers pooled
together. Thread 4 applies {{< acr DER >}}-growth scenarios per archetype
rather than uniformly across a feeder, using exactly the enrichment found
above to decide where growth is likely to bind first.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
